speaker: interviewer
text: Just keep it turned up. I'll get some, some bars.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead-- on-- first door on your left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I've got, got to hang a--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: After this-- Andrew, where should I go?

speaker: interviewee
text: You go--

speaker: interviewer
text: I've got some blue here.

speaker: interviewee
text: --all around, as far as you can go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I need to get a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just don't look at the mess.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a couple of stands out of my, my big kit. In your truck, or-- Yeah. I've, I've got the keys. I'll have to take care of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: --right?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're starting. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm [? Adele ?] [? Shabelsky. ?] And we're here-- I'm interviewing Andrew Meisels for the survivors of the Shoah visual foundation project. Thank you very much for agreeing to allow us to do this interview. Andrew, can you tell us a little bit where you were born, what year you were born, and a little bit about your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on July 19, 1926, in Czechoslovakia, a small town called Munkacs. In Hungarian, it's called Munkacs. In Czechoslovakian, it's called Mukacevo. And the pronunciation is, is what makes the difference. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] that's, that's about-- that's where I grew up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big is the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Town was about 32,000 people-- the total. It was about half and half-- 16,000 Jews, 16,000 non-Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. Did you come from a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father was religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: Orthodox, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a bar mitzvah and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my grandfather had a kaftan, you know, that black jacket with a-- with a, a shtreimel, you know, and white socks like the rabbis get dressed. But my father was just modern, but Orthodox. You know, he-- you couldn't turn on the light on Friday night or Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the whole family observed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me-- tell me who your brothers and sisters-- tell me your parents' names and your brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Bernard. My mother's name was Regina. I had an-- my oldest sister was Margaret. She is still alive. She is-- lives in Montreal now. And then I had an older brother, David. After that, it was me. And after myself-- and there was my younger brother, Ernest, and still another younger brother. He was Tibor. We called him [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the other brothers and sisters have nicknames, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they all had nicknames. [LAUGHS] My brother David had the nickname [NON-ENGLISH]. My, my nickname was [NON-ENGLISH]. My younger brother, Ernest, was [NON-ENGLISH]. And the youngest brother was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So of your four siblings, only your sister, Margaret, survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents also perished, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages were spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Hungarian, really. My, my-- when my parents were born in that region, it used to belong to Hungary. And so we spoke mostly Hungarian and Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were born in 1926, which means you were 13 years old when the--

speaker: interviewee
text: That makes me an old man. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 13 years old when the war broke out. But you were 18, actually, when--

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war broke out in Poland, you mean, in 1938?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, but, but, but, but the Germans didn't invade Hungary until May 1944. So you were already 18. But when you were 13, what do you remember? Do you remember anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going to school. I went to school. I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anyone talking about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were glued to the radio all the time-- what the Germans are doing today, and tomorrow, and the next day, and so on. And the propaganda that they were issuing-- it worked in many different ways. We were listening to the radio when they made a pact with Russia. And then there weren't that many radios in households. We had a lot of neighbors come over and listen to the radio at our house. There was no television, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your parents said about it, about the war? Do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it was frightening that the Germans-- they were spewing hatred, especially against the Jews. And so that's why we listened so intently-- intensely what's going on with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was that the first time that you heard that there was hatred against Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. There was-- I grew up with it. I grew up with it. Because in that small town that we lived in, there were, as I said, 16,000 non-Jews. Many of them were Russian. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And many of them were Schwabs. Schwab is like German. And I was afraid many times just to walk to school because, you know, you-- they beat you up. They were stronger. There were more of them, and so on. So it wasn't the most pleasant childhood that I had as far as growing up and going to school, and so on. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: So even as a small child, you were aware of the anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. Just like I'm finding out here, the colored people are aware of the fact that they are a different skin, which, to me, was a surprise when I came to this country-- that they are treated differently. But that's-- has nothing to do with the interview. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your parents telling you how to deal with the anti-Semitism when you encountered it in school or on the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we dealt with it. We dealt with it. I fought back--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --if I was called names and this and that. I, I was-- I was caught many times in fights in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides that, tell me-- tell us something about your childhood, about your family surrounding.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- we had-- actually, we had a good life. My father had a-- had a, like, a liquor store. And he provided for all of us. We had a comfortable life. Every summer, we went away for, for the entire summer. It was like what you would call here 'vacation.' It, it was in a winery, where it was a lot cooler. And it was just vacationing. There was no school, you know. And I, I always thought it was-- it was a good life. While I said that it was-- it was hard as far as growing up with anti-Semitism, but it was-- it wasn't a difficult life. We, we lived with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- did you feel-- apart from the anti-Semitism, would you feel that you have equal civil and legal rights in, in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah, yes, until later on, you know, when the-- when the war broke out and Czechoslovakia has become Hungary. In 1938, Hitler ordered this part of Czechoslovakia to be returned to Hungary. So instead of Czech school, I started going to Hungarian school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to me, the transition wasn't that difficult because I already spoke Hungarian. And we knew how to read and write Hungarian, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear about Hitler from, let's say, between '33 to '35, or before even '39? Or when, when was the first--

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard about Hitler from the very first-- from 1933, when he was making his, his rise. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the Jewish community afraid that it would affect them in some way? Or they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were. And, as time went on, later on, people came from that region. Either from Poland, they made their escape and told us horrible stories. And we just didn't want to believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm. Is there anything you might want to-- that you remember about your mother and father during those childhood years of-- you mentioned that your father had a liquor store.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother a housewife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she help him in the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. There are a few times I saw her in the store. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides the vacations you would take, do you remember family outings or things you used to do--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was with other-- with other brothers of my father and their families. And so it wasn't just my family alone that went to this winery, and so on. And it was just a lot of fun because there were a lot of us. And as time went on-- I think I was, like, 16 or 17 when my aunt from Romania asked my father to send me one of your boys to help me out. Because she had three sons older. And all three sons were drafted into the Hungarian Army. And here she had a business of a stationary store. And she also had a business of a printing shop. Like, she printed the only placard-- what-- what's playing in the movies next week, and so on, and business cards, books, and whatever a printer is printing. And so she couldn't handle it all. So I volunteered that I will go there. It seemed like an adventure to go to a different town. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was the town from your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really know mileage. That's why I'm going to call my cousin. He-- it, it was a good day's travel by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it wasn't like, like, you can come back anytime, and so on. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sighet.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the same town that Elie Wiesel is from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elie Wiesel is from, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. They were well known. Their name was Kaufman. And they were a, a prominent family in that town. It was also a small town, smaller than Munkacs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the population?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say the percentage of Jews in this town was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, I would say probably half and half.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in both towns, how would you say the, the ethnic groups mixed? Did they mix well? Did they get along?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Isolated?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] When you say 'get along,' I don't exactly know what you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did they coexist, or were they completely isolated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, they coexisted, of course. No, they weren't isolated. They coexisted. And you, you put up a front. But then, then the dislike was down underneath. In many-- in many ways, you could detect that-- not all the time, of course. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any stories of pogroms that circulated in the community, in the Jewish community? Did that-- did that exist in the Jewish consciousness--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --among the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. If, if there were, I'm not aware of it. I, I just don't know. Maybe I was too young to know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your aunt's name who you went to--

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt's name was Kaufman. Her, her name was Janka, J-A-N-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were 17 years old when you went to help her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 17, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was around 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1943. It was  in '43. I was either 16 or 17. I've got to figure it out. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there for-- I was taken from there by the Germans. I wanted to go back home-- to make my way back home as things got worse, and worse, and worse. And by that time, I just couldn't. I learned how to-- how to print [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a, a letter of furlough, for example, for the army. I was making the official stamps for the Hungarian Army there, like the rubber stamp. And, and we printed the furlough papers. That's how her three boys were able to come home many times on a furlough, even though they were not on furlough. They just paid off the sergeant. And he covered for them for a week or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to produce papers like that to help other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. And I printed a paper for myself, like a birth certificate. I did that at 4 o'clock in the morning. No workers were there. I picked out the letters myself. I put-- locked it in the machine. And I printed it. And I made myself a non-Jewish-sounding name. And so therefore, I figured I'm able to travel on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were taking off Jews from the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do that for anyone else?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- nobody knew that. And I didn't do it for anyone except for myself. And my aunt found out. She started to cry. She wouldn't let me go. She said, you're the only man in the family. And you heard what the Germans are-- this is already later on, when the Germans were promising you this-- that we're all taking you to one town somewhere in Hungary where all the Jews will be assembled in that one town. And so you'll meet your parents there at that time. I just wanted to make my way back home to Munkacs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's back up for a moment. How long were you with your aunt before the Germans invaded Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about a year-- about a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you hadn't been home for a year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hadn't been home at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the Germans entered Hungary in May 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they entered-- I think it was in March. They-- but the directives that were issued maybe two weeks later-- all the Jews have to wear an arm band. Another week later, all the Jews have to wear a, a-- the Star, Star of David.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you wear a star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: And all the Jews have to move to another part of town, which was the ghetto. Nobody was allowed in or out of there. And it just so happens I was allowed to go out because, as I said, I was printing for the Hungarian Army all kinds of papers that they needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did the ghetto have a name? Or was it just several streets that were secluded?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just several streets that were secluded, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when those-- when that ghetto was liquidated, or evacuated, put on trains, was the whole area evacuated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that done within one day or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really know. I know that we had to go out in the middle of the street one day. It was in early May. And a few hours later, they loaded us onto boxcars. And three days later, I wound up in a prison camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any warning? Did they say, you had-- you would be leaving at 5:00 in the morning or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or they just came around and said--

speaker: interviewee
text: They just came around. And, and it wasn't even the, the, the Hungarian police. The gendarmes, they were the worst. They were worse than, than the Germans themselves. They brought in police from different cities so that you don't know privately these people that you are dealing with right now, and so on. They were just terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why, why do you think they were worse?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Because they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm asking you to speculate now.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were hitting-- beating you with the gun butts. Just, just making it, you know-- they didn't have to do these things on their own. Nobody was behind them, watching them, and so on. They just did it because they were sadistic. They were happy to see the Jews get out of there. I'm just adding that as a guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the deportation, did you try to have contact with your family? Or was it impossible?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not possible--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: --just--

speaker: interviewer
text: --get word from them either?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In the beginning, we were writing to each other, you know. My mother was very proud of me because my aunt wrote all kinds of nice things about me. My aunt was very religious. You couldn't even go to a movie. And I was working-- I loved what I learned in the printing shop. And I was working there on my own. I did things on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Finally, she said, why don't you go have some fun? I mean, you're always working, working. Why don't you take a girl out, go to movies? And when [LAUGHS] my family found out that my aunt asked me to go to the movies, it was big news that-- Were you deported with here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many of you-- you were able to travel together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she had her daughter-in-law there. She had a little boy, three years old-- the daughter-in-law-- who loved me to pieces. That's one of the reasons why she was able to convince me not to leave. And they're going to take you off the train. I said, no, they're not. I'm not a Jew. And I pulled out my paper. And I showed her what I printed for myself. And then she even got scared worse. They're going to catch you. They're going to shoot you right there. And so I told her, chances have to be taken. I want to get home. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Weren't you afraid, yourself, that you would be found out?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It-- it's not that difficult to prove that someone is a Jew, especially a man.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was afraid. But still, you do things even though you are afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you-- if the end-- the goal is you want it that bad-- and I wanted to go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember from the time when, when the town was being round up for the deportations?

speaker: interviewee
text: All I remember was a lot of shouting and yelling and screaming, and whatnot. And the three boys that my aunt had that were drafted in the army-- the youngest one heard what's going on, and so on. And he, he made his way home through one of those furlough papers that I printed. He wanted to say goodbye to his girlfriend. He was about three years older than I was, maybe, maybe four. And the Germans caught him. And they threw him in the-- in the boxcars. They beat him up and threw him in the boxcars. He wound up in, in Auschwitz, also. In fact, he had the number-- my number is 4344. His number was 4345. And his brother-in-law's number-- he had older sisters that were married-- his brother-in-law's number was 4343. He was with me in the camp all throughout-- almost to the end. The reason I say almost-- we got separated in Buchenwald. That was to-- almost near the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he lives in Haifa, Israel. I went to visit him. He was very happy to see me. And, and this younger-- the younger son that came to say goodbye to his family, he never made it. After three weeks, we got separated in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: The boxcars-- how many people in each boxcar?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how many people there were, but we were packed in like herrings practically. Just-- it was just suffocating, hot, smelly. They-- you had your bathroom right there. And you didn't go out. They didn't ever open the doors. And if somebody was screaming that somebody had died or something, they still didn't open the doors. So it, it was just a horrible trip that I, I want to forget. I can't. I don't want to forget. But I want to forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any water to drink on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think they gave you water. We had-- we had some food that we were allowed to bring with us. But they didn't give us any food.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three days-- three days and two nights, two nights and three days, something like that. Or maybe three nights and two days. I don't remember. It took a total of three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you arrived in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in Birkenau. We arrived into a camp, where we're [SIGHS] surrounded by barbed wire. And as you got off the train-- and everybody was, schnell, schnell! Los, los! Everything had to be done fast. I don't care how fast you did something, it wasn't fast enough for the Germans. And and there was a-- one person that looked like a ranked German, with high rank. And he was telling you, you go to this side. You go to this side, and so on. And later on, we found out the left side meant-- or one side meant life. The other side meant the death camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How soon did you find out that's what it meant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, as soon as we, we got inside. I was inside the camp already with a striped Sing-Sing type of suit as a prisoner. One of the barrack leaders, who was a Polish Jew, also a prisoner-- but he was already there since 1938 or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, you see that big flame up there? And he said, that's your parents burning there. Because the smell was-- I mean, I smelled in my life bad smells at times-- cow manure, or whatever. It was a bad smell. This was the worst smell that I have ever smelled in my whole life. It was just terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, that's your parents burning there. And I just-- I said, you're crazy! He said, yeah. I spit in his face. I literally spit in his face. He started beating me up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he trying to be cruel when he told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, he was. And I even yelled at him. I said, why are you saying that? You're a Jew. I mean, why, why are you saying these things?

speaker: interviewer
text: And I ran out of the barrack. He ran after me. Pretty soon, four, four SS Germans came by and said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. What's going on here? So he was telling me-- he was them that I spit on him. And that's why I beat him. He didn't say the reason why, and so on. So the Germans started to beat me. And I passed out. [CRYING] I'm sorry. I passed out about three times. And they poured water on me just to revive me. And as a punishment, he says, you go with this transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the reason they left me there-- they would've beat me to death. All of a sudden, it, it just started pouring rain out of the clear blue. It just was raining. And they started to get wet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they just left me there. And one of them yelled back the order, take him with you on that transport. And then I was told as a punishment, you're going to Auschwitz. Oh my god, how much worse can it be than this here? I was happy to get out of there. This is two, three days after arrival?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. In Auschwitz, the very first day, they tattooed me, which wasn't a picnic in itself either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have permanent injuries from that first beating in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have permanent injuries. Fortunately, the, the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the leader of the barrack-- he picked up a shovel. And he was beating me with the handle of the shovel. And at one point, he hit me right here on the bone. And the shovel broke-- the handle. So he just left me. And just at that time is when the Germans arrived. One of the reasons why I wound up there in that barrack-- as I said, I was taken from Sighet. I wound up among total strangers. They spoke in Romanian among themselves. I didn't even understand what they're talking about. Now, they were Jews, mind you. But they spoke Romanian. And here and there, I heard some Jewish words. And I said to myself, I'm just going to die here. I have to find some people from my hometown. Because word gets around that people arrived yesterday from Munkacs-- or today, and so on. And I go from barrack to barrack to barrack looking for some of the people that I am familiar with. And sure enough, I found-- I found a barrack with-- full of kids from Munkacs, classmates of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided to stay there. And I was so naive. I was just a young kid. And, I mean, what did I know? You were 17--

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple hours later--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was 17, yeah. A couple of hours later, some high-ranking [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people came in. And they make an announcement that one person-- there's, there's one person too many in this barrack. And from barrack number so-and-so, there's one person missing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Obviously, this person would like to remain here. Otherwise, he wouldn't have come here. We're going to let him stay here. All we want to do is just get assurance that indeed, that's what happened. Nothing will happen to him. Will he please step forward? And I was stupid enough to step forward. And that's when that-- the barrack leader was telling me, you see that big fire? That's your parents burning there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he started-- and that's when the Germans started hitting me. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I said, as, as a punishment, I went to Auschwitz, where they tattooed me. I was there for three weeks just doing nothing. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Andrew, before you tell me about Auschwitz, we need to take a short break to change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw him tap you on the shoulder. [LAUGHS]
